ptp.o: Assault — s.2 NFOAP 1997
e: Directly or indirectly applied force to / caused impact on the other person, OR caused them to believe on reasonable grounds they were likely immediately to be subjected to such force
Without lawful excuse
Without the consent of the other
Intentionally or recklessly
ptp.a: Summary only. Not arrestable of itself — consider s.24 POA arrest powers / public order context.

ptp.o: Assault causing harm — s.3 NFOAP 1997 (as amended)
e: An assault (as s.2)
That caused harm: injury to body or mind, incl. pain and unconsciousness
Intent/recklessness applies to the assault — no need to prove intent to cause the harm
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 10 yrs since 2023 amendment — verify).

ptp.o: Causing serious harm — s.4 NFOAP 1997
e: Intentionally or recklessly
Caused serious harm: injury creating substantial risk of death, or serious disfigurement, or substantial loss/impairment of mobility or of a bodily member/organ function
ptp.a: Arrestable (max life).

ptp.o: Threats to kill or cause serious harm — s.5 NFOAP 1997
e: Made a threat, by any means, to kill or cause serious harm
To that person or a third person
Intending the recipient to believe it would be carried out
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 10 yrs).

ptp.o: Harassment — s.10 NFOAP 1997
e: By any means, persistently followed, watched, pestered, besetted or communicated with/about the person
Acts done intentionally or recklessly
Seriously interfered with peace and privacy OR caused alarm, distress or harm
A reasonable person would realise the acts would have that effect
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 10 yrs).

ptp.o: Theft — s.4 CJ(T&FO)A 2001
e: Dishonestly (without a claim of right made in good faith)
Appropriated property
Without the consent of its owner
With the intention of depriving its owner of it
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 10 yrs).

ptp.o: Burglary — s.12 CJ(T&FO)A 2001
e: Entered a building or part of a building
As a trespasser (knowing or reckless as to trespass)
With intent to commit an arrestable offence therein — OR having entered, committed/attempted arrestable offence
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 14 yrs). Aggravated burglary s.13 if weapon.

ptp.o: Robbery — s.14 CJ(T&FO)A 2001
e: Stole (all elements of theft)
And immediately before or at the time, used force on any person, or put/sought to put any person in fear of immediate force
In order to steal
ptp.a: Arrestable (max life).

ptp.o: Making gain / causing loss by deception — s.6 CJ(T&FO)A 2001
e: Dishonestly
With intent to make a gain for self/another or cause loss to another
By deception, induced someone to do or refrain from doing an act
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 5 yrs).

ptp.o: Handling stolen property — s.17 CJ(T&FO)A 2001
e: Property was stolen
Knowing it was stolen or being reckless as to whether it was
Dishonestly received it, or undertook/assisted in its retention, removal, disposal or realisation for another's benefit
Otherwise than in the course of stealing
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 10 yrs).

ptp.o: Criminal damage — s.2 CDA 1991
e: Damaged property (incl. temporarily impairing usefulness/value)
Belonging to another (or own property with intent to defraud)
Without lawful excuse
Intentionally or recklessly
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 10 yrs; arson/endangering life higher).

ptp.o: Possession of controlled drug — s.3 MDA 1977
e: Substance was a controlled drug (certificate of analysis)
In the person's possession — physical control + knowledge
Possession unlawful (no prescription/licence)
ptp.a: Cannabis 1st/2nd: consider Adult Caution. s.3 simple possession not arrestable of itself — powers via s.23/25 MDA.

ptp.o: Possession for sale or supply — s.15 MDA 1977
e: All elements of s.3 possession
With intent to sell or otherwise supply to another
s.15A: market value €13,000+ (presumptive minimum 10 yrs)
ptp.a: Arrestable (max life). Quantity, cash, baggies, tick lists, phones = intent evidence.

ptp.o: Intoxication in public place — s.4 CJ(PO)A 1994
e: In a public place
Intoxicated (alcohol/drug/solvent or combination)
To such an extent as would give rise to a reasonable apprehension of danger to self or others
ptp.a: Fixed charge option. s.24 arrest power on failure to comply/give details.

ptp.o: Threatening, abusive, insulting behaviour — s.6 CJ(PO)A 1994
e: In a public place
Used or engaged in threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour
With intent to provoke a breach of the peace, or being reckless as to whether one may be occasioned
ptp.a: Fixed charge option. Record exact words used.

ptp.o: Failure to comply with direction — s.8 CJ(PO)A 1994
e: Garda found person in a public place
Reasonable suspicion of s.4/5/6/9 conduct or loitering in circumstances giving reasonable apprehension for safety/property/peace
Direction given: to desist and/or leave immediately in a peaceable orderly manner
Direction was clear, and person failed to comply without lawful authority or reasonable excuse
ptp.a: s.24 arrest. Record the exact direction verbatim.

ptp.o: Breach of DV order — s.33 DVA 2018
e: A safety/barring/interim/emergency/protection order was in force
Respondent had been notified of it
Contravened its terms
ptp.a: Arrest without warrant on reasonable cause — s.35 DVA 2018. Always get order details + service proof.

ptp.o: Coercive control — s.39 DVA 2018
e: Knowingly and persistently engaged in controlling/coercive behaviour
Against a relevant person (intimate relationship)
Behaviour had a serious effect: fear of violence, or serious alarm/distress with substantial adverse impact on day-to-day activities
A reasonable person would consider it likely to have that effect
ptp.a: Arrestable (max 5 yrs). Pattern evidence — diary, messages, witnesses, finances.

ptp.o: Unauthorised taking of MPV — s.112 RTA 1961
e: Used or took possession of a mechanically propelled vehicle
Without the consent of the owner or other lawful authority
(Or knowingly carried in such vehicle)
ptp.a: Arrestable. Note: intent to deprive permanently → theft instead.

ptp.o: Dangerous driving — s.53 RTA 1961
e: Drove a vehicle in a public place
In a manner (incl. speed) dangerous to the public
Having regard to all circumstances: place, traffic actual/expected
ptp.a: Arrestable if death/serious harm caused. Preserve dashcam/CCTV immediately.

must: Identification of assailant: known? how long/how well? If stranger — full ADVOKATE
Exact mechanics: each blow/kick/weapon, which hand, where it landed, how many times, sequence
Words spoken before/during/after — verbatim in quotes
Consent expressly negatived: 'I did not agree to any of this'
Harm: every injury incl. pain, bruising visible later, unconsciousness even momentary; ongoing effects (s.3)
Serious harm (s.4): treatment, prognosis, risk to life, disfigurement, loss of function — anchor to medical evidence
Fear (s.2 apprehension limb): what they believed was about to happen and why
Sobriety of witness, lighting, distance, duration of view
Medical: doctor/hospital attended, consent to obtain records (separate consent form)
Photos of injuries: when taken, by whom — and repeat at 48-72h when bruising peaks
Clothing/weapons: where now, offer as exhibits
CCTV/phone footage: what exists, who holds it
Prior incidents/threats with same person
Impact: work missed, fear, sleep, effect on daily life (feeds VIS later)
Ownership: 'I am the owner of…' or capacity (employee authorised to speak for owner)
Description of property: make, model, serial/IMEI, unique marks, value and how valued
Consent negatived expressly: 'I did not give anyone permission to take it'
Last seen / discovered missing: exact times and place — narrows the window
Who had access; keys/alarms/locks state
Recovery details if any: condition, missing parts
For handling: how accused came to possess, price paid vs value, circumstances suggesting knowledge
CCTV: locations and retention deadlines
Receipts/proof of purchase as exhibits
Insurance claim status (credibility — disclose it)
Premises: dwelling or other building, who lives/works there, occupier's capacity to speak
Trespass negatived: 'No one had permission to enter'
Point and method of entry: damage, tool marks — photograph before repair
State of premises before vs after — rooms disturbed, search pattern
Property taken: full theft-style description per item
Occupancy at the time — anyone home, what they heard/saw, fear caused
Scene preservation: what was touched after discovery, by whom (elimination prints/DNA)
Alarm/lock/phone-line interference
CCTV incl. neighbours' doorbells — canvass radius
Impact on household — especially dwelling burglary (aggravating)
Everything in theft guide PLUS:
Force or threat: exact acts and words, weapon description held how, distance
Fear: 'I believed he would… I was in fear of…' — the subjective apprehension, and why it was reasonable
Timing: force immediately before/at the time and in order to steal — make the sequence explicit
Injuries even minor — bridges to s.3 if needed
Description of suspect: full ADVOKATE, direction of flight, vehicle
Property handed over vs taken — how it left their control
Witnesses present and their positions
Finding member: exact location of drugs (which pocket, under which seat), suspect's proximity and reaction
Words said on finding — verbatim, cautioned or not, note which
Continuity: exhibit ref, seal number, who held it to PEMS
Indicia of supply: cash denominations, weighing scales, baggies, tick lists, multiple phones — itemise
Observations pre-stop: exchanges seen, duration, positions
Occupier statements for premises: who controls which rooms
Certificate of analysis will prove the substance — statement proves possession and knowledge
s.15A: seizure of full quantity, valuation member's basis
Relationship history and status — establishes 'relevant person'
The order: type, date, court, terms, respondent's knowledge of it
The breach/incident: exact conduct against exact term
History of prior incidents — pattern (essential for coercive control)
Coercive control: finances, isolation, surveillance, threats, degradation — concrete examples with dates, effect on daily life
Children present: names, ages, what they witnessed
Fear and its basis; safety concerns going forward (informs bail objection)
Injuries per assault guide; photos with repeat at 48-72h
Digital evidence: texts, calls, social media — screenshots with dates, offer phone for download
Statement withdrawal risk: record thoroughly enough to prosecute without her if needed (s.16 CJA 2006 potential) — video statement consideration
Supports offered and refuge/safety plan noted separately, not in statement
Time, exact location, public place established
Crowd/context: numbers, mood, passers-by present
Exact words and behaviour — verbatim quotes
For s.6: intent/recklessness re breach of peace — the surrounding aggression
For s.8: reasonable suspicion grounds, the direction word-for-word, warning of arrest, response, time allowed to comply
Intoxication signs itemised: smell, speech, gait, eyes, conduct
Danger apprehended: to whom and why
Civilian witnesses: effect on them — fear, alarm, having to move away
Bodycam/CCTV reference
The deception precisely: what was represented, by whom, how (words/documents/conduct)
Why it was false and that accused knew/was reckless
Inducement: 'Because of this I…' — the act done in reliance
Gain/loss quantified with documents
Timeline of contacts — calls, emails, meetings
Documents as exhibits: contracts, transfers, screenshots, bank records (consent for bank enquiries)
Attempts to recover / contact after discovery
Other victims known
Each incident separately: date, time, place, exact conduct — persistence is the offence
Means: following, watching, communications — platform, account names, screenshots preserved
Any response asked to stop — and continuation after
Effect: interference with peace/privacy, alarm, distress — concrete life changes
Third-party witnesses to incidents
Prior relationship/context
Preservation: phone downloads consent, account details for service providers
Public place, direction, road/weather/light conditions
The driving observed: speeds, manoeuvres, near-misses — moment by moment
Vehicle and driver identification — nomination, admissions at scene
Injuries and damage; positions of vehicles after
Scene measurements/marks, photos, PSV inspector requested
Independent witnesses and dashcams — canvass immediately
Anything said by driver — cautioned admissions
Specialist interview (protected disclosure / SAIU) — first account brief, don't contaminate
First complaint: who told, when, exact words used — recent complaint evidence
Preserve: clothing unwashed in paper bags, no shower where possible before SATU
SATU referral within forensic window
Consent: what was said and done to communicate non-consent; incapacity (drink/sleep) facts
Scene and digital preservation immediately
Support: rape crisis contact, special measures explained
Suspect's account locked in early via interview

major.t: Major incident & serious crime — first response guide

items: Life first: casualties, ambulance, render aid — note everything you move
Arrest if suspect present; note res gestae utterances verbatim
Cordons: inner (scene) + outer (control) — wider than feels necessary
One entry route; scene log commenced — every person, time in/out, purpose
Nothing else touched; weather protection improvised without contaminating
First descriptions circulated: suspect, vehicle, direction
Dying declaration: if death imminent and declarant aware — record exact words, witnesses
Member i/c appointed and identified to all
SOCO/FSI requested; nothing processed before their direction
Common approach path marked
Vehicles, weapons, phones: in situ until photographed
Body: not moved until directed — pathologist for suspicious deaths
Log every decision with time and reason (this becomes your evidence)
Identify, separate, obtain first accounts and full details before they disperse
House-to-house parameters set and logged
CCTV: harvest radius, all routes to/from, RETENTION REQUESTS SAME DAY (Braddish/Dunne duty)
Dashcam appeal, taxis, buses, doorbells
Significant witnesses: statements by experienced members, consider video
Arrest strategy: timing vs evidence readiness — detention clock starts on arrest
Preserve suspect forensics: clothing, hands (firearms residue), phone seized and airplane-mode/faraday
Interview plan: disclosure strategy, inference provisions prepared properly
Searches: s.10 CJA 2006 / offence-specific warrants — get them right first time (Damache)
SIO appointed; incident room established
Jobs book: every action numbered, allocated, resulted
Exhibits officer single point; PEMS discipline from hour one
Family liaison appointed for homicide — one channel, logged contact
Media via press office only; holding lines agreed
Regular conferences: decisions logged with rationale
CCTV lost to overwrite — request preservation in writing day one
Scene released too early — you cannot go back
First accounts not recorded — memory contaminates fast
Continuity gaps in exhibits — every handover recorded
Detention clock mismanaged — extensions sought late
Disclosure duty ignored during investigation — schedule as you go
